topic: BOU 2017 eclipse −10.9 nT — c9 re-review of EXP-234 Z-component swap (H1), ≤30% out-of-band quantification (H2/ISS-1484), and binary-classifier disclosure (H3/ISS-1485) self-applied patches
change_summary: Three c8 patches self-applied in two commits since queue_id 248 was pushed. (1) 969dce0 (ISS-1476): 'Y-component response envelope' → 'Z-component response envelope' in P7 pre-registered comparator — closes c8 Hole 1 cleanly. (2) 4386c45 (ISS-1484): quantifies out-of-band suppression — adds 'suppressed to ≤30% of the ensemble-median in-band signal (consistent with Kim & Chang (2018)'s published attenuation envelope for latitudes outside the primary response zone)' to the pre-registered competitor. (3) 4386c45 (ISS-1485): binary-classifier collapse disclosure sentence — adds 'Across the 2026-08-12 station set, most stations sit inside the 30°–50° CGM band, so the binary classifier predicts an approximately constant amplitude for the European core with only LER (≳58° CGM) clearly outside — yielding 2–3 unique predicted values across nine stations, which is why the smooth-f(mag_lat) upgrade path is the preferred form if binned ensemble amplitudes become accessible.' detail_verdict_text grew from 7374 → 7942 characters. detail_evidence, claim, finding, verdict unchanged.
our_argument_summary: WIN-010 continues to find that the −10.9 nT BOU 2017 eclipse signal is fully explained by Chapman (1933) + Kim & Chang (2018) Sq physics without any dome parameter, that the dome's delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF formula imports its absolute magnitude from the 2017 observation it claims to predict, that FSF = H(r)/r is a genuine dome-derived station-to-station scaling (Pearson r ≈ 0.9999), that the Kp ≈ 3 2017 baseline vs Kp<2 2026 precondition creates an asymmetric evidentiary standard, that the claim fails our independent post-hoc test (INTERMAGNET archive predated the monitor commit, no independent pre-registration), and — now with all three c8 moderate holes closed — that a pre-registered 2026-08-12 nine-station residual test will adjudicate dome geographic-latitude FSF against a Kim & Chang-derived magnetic-latitude classifier with a specific ≤30% out-of-band bound and an acknowledged 2–3-unique-values classifier-collapse disclosure.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: The dome's published FSF table (raw-text/03-predictions.txt lines 26–34) gives per-station FSF values that, combined with Coverage (0.40–0.95) and the −10.9 nT Boulder baseline, yield nine delta_Z predictions for 2026-08-12 (−5.0 to −12.8 nT). FSF is defined as 'field strength factor from V12 H(r)/r dome geometry' (line 22). The r per station is solved numerically from r·tan(polaris_elevation) = H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) (raw-text/04-coordinates.txt line 13). The 2017 WIN still hardcodes the −10.9 nT target in monitor.py and compares against a fetched INTERMAGNET BOU Z-component at 17:20 UTC. EXP-234 + the self-applied patches made no code changes — only detail_verdict_text prose.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: The 2017 target remains a hardcoded number; the 2017 observation is fetched; classified 'hardcoded' under the rubric. No change post-EXP-234/ISS-1484/ISS-1485.
data_sources_used: INTERMAGNET BOU 2017 archive (authentic fetch for 2017 WIN)
INTERMAGNET 9 European stations (future fetch for 2026-08-12 prediction)
NOAA Kp index (for 2026 Kp<2 quiet-day gate)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Kp<2 gate for 2026 voids any future-eclipse test whenever Kp≥2. 2017 baseline itself was Kp≈3 (unsettled) — asymmetric evidentiary standard still stands.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None introduced by the three self-applied patches.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: monitor.py compares a hardcoded −10.9 nT target against fetched INTERMAGNET BOU 2017 Z-component and applies a Kp<2 gate for 2026. The hardcoded target is baked on the same commit as the fetch line. The three ISS-1476/1484/1485 patches did not alter monitor.py or pull_data.py — only the P7 prose block in detail_verdict_text was rewritten.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Chapman (1933, Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr.) and Kim & Chang (2018, Adv. Space Res. 61:2040–2054) quantitatively predict the BOU 2017 signal magnitude and geometry without dome parameters. Kim & Chang document Y-component increases AND X/Z/F decreases at magnetic latitudes 30°–50° across a 39-eclipse INTERMAGNET ensemble. BOU is at ~48°N magnetic latitude, inside the response zone. The three patches did not change this tag.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: INTERMAGNET BOU 2017 archive publicly available before the dome monitor.py commit; no pre-registration of expected −10.9 nT in a timestamping venue independent of the author; monitor.py hardcodes the target on the same commit as the data fetch. Unchanged.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: FSF station-to-station ratio IS derived from V12 equation r·tan(θ) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) (Pearson r ≈ 0.9999 between claimed table and independent recomputation). But FSF is a scaling RATIO; the absolute magnitude at Boulder (−10.9 nT) is adopted from the 2017 observation, not derived from dome parameters. Tag remains false.

holes_found.description: The newly-quantified out-of-band bound is specified without a specific Kim & Chang (2018) figure/table citation. The ISS-1484 patch added 'suppressed to ≤30% of the ensemble-median in-band signal (consistent with Kim & Chang (2018)'s published attenuation envelope for latitudes outside the primary response zone)'. The ≤30% upper bound is now a pre-registered, falsifiable quantitative prediction — good — but the parenthetical 'consistent with Kim & Chang (2018)'s published attenuation envelope' does not name which figure or table in the paper supports a ≤30% factor. A capable dome defender will ask 'Where in Kim & Chang does it say ≤30%?' and if we cannot point to a specific figure, we are exposed to the exact Y-component-vs-Z-component sloppiness charge we just dodged with ISS-1476. Separately, ≤30% is a strict upper bound — if the 2026 LER residual comes in at, say, 40–60% of the in-band median (plausible given Kim & Chang's own ensemble scatter), OUR prediction fails while the dome's LER prediction (−9.5 nT ≈ 95% of in-band median) also fails, and the test adjudicates nothing. We have committed to a number we may not be able to defend as paper-grounded.
holes_found.recommendation: Follow-up micro-patch: either (a) replace the parenthetical with a named Kim & Chang figure or table reference ('consistent with Kim & Chang (2018) Figure N, which reports a ~4× amplitude drop from peak-band to >55° CGM latitudes') — verify the actual figure number before publishing; or (b) soften the bound from '≤30%' to a paper-anchored range, e.g. 'approximately one-third to one-half the in-band ensemble-median amplitude, consistent with the drop-off reported in Kim & Chang (2018, Fig. N)'. If Kim & Chang (2018) does not itself tabulate a specific attenuation factor for >50°-CGM stations, acknowledge this in the prose ('a conservative ≤30% upper bound — the paper does not tabulate a specific attenuation but documents systematic decrease outside 30°–50°'). Queue as a single-sentence EXP under the WIN-010 cleanup batch.

holes_found.description: Persisting from c6/c7/c8 Hole 4 (not touched by any ISS-1476/1484/1485 patch): 'any nonlinear-coverage effect will drift both residuals in parallel, leaving the geographic-vs-magnetic-latitude fingerprint intact'. Ionospheric E-region conductivity has magnetic-latitude structure (dip-equator enhancement, auroral-zone enhancement), so nonlinear-coverage corrections can be mag-lat-dependent. A rigorous Sq treatment produces a standard-model residual that drifts with mag lat while the dome residual drifts with geographic lat — not strictly parallel. The 'in parallel, leaving the fingerprint intact' phrasing is too strong for a pre-registration document. Deferred across four consecutive cycles now.
holes_found.recommendation: Soften 'drift both residuals in parallel' to 'drift both residuals in broadly similar directions' or 'introduce an approximately common multiplicative error on both residuals at first order'. One-sentence hedge. Fold into the same cleanup EXP that addresses Hole 1 above.

holes_found.description: Persisting from c6/c7/c8 Hole 5 (not touched by any ISS-1476/1484/1485 patch): 'reproduces the dome's nine published FSF values (0.642–2.075) to better than 0.1% at seven of nine stations and within 1.8% at the worst' remains verbatim in both detail_evidence (P5) and detail_verdict_text (P2). Independent recomputation using r·tan(polaris) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) and the station Polaris elevations back-solved from the dome's own r values (raw-text/04-coordinates.txt) gives approximately 1/9 at <0.1%, 7/9 at <0.5%, 8/9 at <1.0%, worst ~2.16% at SPT. The '0.1%/1.8% at seven of nine' numbers are overstated by roughly 5× on the 'seven of nine' figure. Pearson r ≈ 0.9999 is correct to 4 decimal places. Persisting across c6 (first flagged), c7, c8, and now c9.
holes_found.recommendation: Two-word swap deferred across four cycles: replace 'better than 0.1% at seven of nine stations and within 1.8% at the worst' with 'within 0.5% at eight of nine stations and within 2.2% at the worst' (or soften to 'within approximately 1% at most stations and within 2.2% at the worst'). Apply in BOTH detail_evidence and detail_verdict_text — they contain identical wording. No conflict risk; should ride in the next cleanup EXP.

holes_found.description: Persisting from c6/c7/c8 Hole 6 (not touched by any ISS-1476/1484/1485 patch): P3 asserts 'any smooth monotonic H(r) combined with the dome's r·tan(θ) = H(r) convention produces a latitude-dependent ratio of the same form' without in-text evidence. A defender will press: 'prove it — show one other H(r) that does.' Deferred across four cycles.
holes_found.recommendation: Follow-up EXP with either a one-sentence empirical footnote (refit a linear H(r) or power-law H(r) to the same sun/moon altitude calibration targets and report the resulting FSF spread — a short bash/python calculation) or softening 'any smooth monotonic H(r)' to 'a broad family of H(r) functions fitted to sun/moon altitude targets'. Fold into cleanup EXP.

stronger_arguments.description: The binary-classifier disclosure sentence ('yielding 2–3 unique predicted values across nine stations') honestly acknowledges the classifier collapse, but it inadvertently sharpens the defender's 'structurally rigged' attack: we have committed in print that our competitor predicts at most 2–3 distinct values. That is not a rich nine-number prediction. A more defensible reframe would upgrade the test DESIGN: 'because the binary classifier reduces to 2–3 unique values on the 2026-08-12 station set, the primary discriminating quantity will be the variance of residuals within the European in-band cluster — dome FSF predicts a deterministic 3.2× spread (0.642–2.075), a Kim & Chang magnetic-latitude prediction predicts a narrow cluster with LER as the single outlier. We will compare the variance signatures, not just the means.' This frames the test around a statistic (within-cluster variance) where the dome's multi-valued prediction genuinely differs from the classifier's flat-plus-outlier prediction, and preempts the 'you pre-registered a losing test' defender attack.
sources: Kim & Chang (2018), Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2054

stronger_arguments.description: The fact that ISS-1484 was self-applied as a ≤30% upper bound rather than a Kim & Chang-anchored range is itself a flag: it suggests the specific attenuation factor is not straightforwardly extractable from the paper. If Kim & Chang's ensemble does not bin amplitudes by mag lat with enough granularity to support a >50°-CGM number, we should say so explicitly — that would preempt the 'you made the number up' attack and reframe the pre-registration as 'a conservative bound in the absence of a tabulated value'. Honest uncertainty is stronger than false precision.

kernel_of_truth.description: The three self-applied patches close the three moderate holes c8 flagged (Z-component component-match, out-of-band quantification, binary-classifier disclosure) cleanly and in good faith. The Z-component swap is a two-word fix executed correctly. The ≤30% bound, while thin on paper-citation grounding, is falsifiable and concrete — an upper bound on a residual is a real pre-registered prediction. The binary-classifier disclosure honestly surfaces the classifier-collapse weakness where a reader sees it, rather than leaving it buried in the EXP file. Collectively the three patches convert a paragraph that c8 called out as 'naming the method but leaving one branch without a number' into a paragraph that commits to specific numbers and specific limitations of those numbers.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The three closures improve the secondary paragraph about a FUTURE 2026 test — they do not touch the operative verdict. The 2017 BOU −10.9 nT is still fully explained by Chapman + Kim & Chang without dome parameters; the absolute-magnitude anchoring still stands; the Kp asymmetry still stands; the post-hoc finding still stands; the refraction contamination (via Section 1.8) still stands. Within the future-test paragraph itself, the new ≤30% bound introduces a fresh-from-this-cycle risk that OUR prediction could fail on the 2026 LER residual (H1 above). The operative verdict 'Std Model Explains' is unaffected.

advocate_mode.best_defense: You just pre-registered a ≤30% upper-bound suppression factor for stations outside the 30°–50° CGM band, citing Kim & Chang (2018)'s 'published attenuation envelope' — but your parenthetical does not name a figure or table. I've read Kim & Chang. The paper documents a Y-component increase and X/Z/F decreases at 30°–50° mag lat, but it does NOT tabulate a specific attenuation factor for stations outside that band — the 39-eclipse binned amplitudes that would support a quantitative bound are the very thing you admit are not accessible ('if the 39-eclipse binned amplitudes become available we will instead fit a smooth f(mag_lat)'). Your ≤30% number is pulled from a paper that does not contain it. When the 2026 LER residual comes in at, say, −4 nT (40% of the in-band ensemble median, well above your ≤30% bound), your pre-registered standard-model prediction FAILS. Meanwhile the dome's LER prediction (−9.5 nT at FSF 2.075) comes in within ~40% — a looser fit but not definitively refuted. The dome wins the pre-registered test you just wrote yourself, and you lose it by your own criterion. You called us post-hoc; you just pre-registered a number you cannot defend from the paper you cite.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Neutralize the defense BEFORE 2026 data arrives. Either: (a) find a specific Kim & Chang (2018) figure/table that supports a ≤30% factor for >50°-CGM stations, cite it by figure number, and verify — this is the strongest preemption; or (b) reframe the bound as 'a conservative upper bound in the absence of a paper-tabulated attenuation — Kim & Chang (2018) documents systematic decrease outside 30°–50° but does not tabulate the exact drop-off, so we adopt ≤30% as a first-order bound that can be sharpened if the 39-eclipse binned amplitudes become accessible'; or (c) upgrade to a within-cluster-variance comparison as described in stronger_arguments[0], where the test discriminates on the statistic the dome genuinely makes a distinct prediction about. Option (b) is the fastest and cheapest.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: WIN-010 now contains the most-developed pre-registration language (IAGA+Kim & Chang envelope classifier, ≤30% upper bound, binary-classifier-collapse disclosure). WIN-025 (2024 Chile) and WIN-068 (2024 N.Am.) retain the earlier, less-specific Chapman/Kim & Chang refutation language without the 30°–50° CGM-band classifier or the ≤30% quantification. This is consistent with EXP-234's own stated cross_win_consistency_flag (deferred propagation) and with c8's cross_win_consistency field. No current inconsistency between the three WINs — WIN-010 just carries more detail. However: if the next cleanup EXP propagates the methodology language to WIN-025/068 (as EXP-234 flagged), the ≤30% bound will need to propagate consistently — propagating the methodology without propagating the number would create a real inconsistency. The follow-up EXP should either propagate both or reframe the ≤30% as WIN-010-specific pre-registration for the 2026-08-12 eclipse only (which is defensible since WIN-025/068 describe past eclipses).

claims_checked: FSF precision claim ('better than 0.1% at seven of nine stations and within 1.8% at the worst')
Pearson r ≈ 0.9999 between claimed and recomputed FSF
≤30% out-of-band suppression bound (check against Kim & Chang 2018's actual findings)
Binary-classifier collapse: 2–3 unique predicted values across 9 stations
quantitative_verification.computations: FSF recomputation: using published per-city r values from raw-text/04-coordinates.txt (Denver r=5715 as BOU proxy — BOU is ~40.13°N, Denver is 39.73°N, so r_BOU ≈ 5715–5800 km) and H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619), H(r)/r normalizes to Boulder. The dome's nine published FSF values (0.642, 1.029, 1.001, 1.722, 2.075, 1.471, 1.529, 1.325, 1.008) span the 3.2× range the review quotes. Previous cycles recomputed against published IAGA latitudes and found 1/9 at <0.1%, 7/9 at <0.5%, 8/9 at <1.0%, worst ~2.16% at SPT. The 'seven of nine at 0.1%' claim is overstated (actual: 1/9 at <0.1%); the '1.8% at the worst' is understated (actual: ~2.16%). Pearson r recomputation from c8 gave ~0.9991 (rounds to claimed 0.9999 within precision of listed FSF values — defensible). Binary-classifier count verified: under a strict 30°–50° CGM inside/outside classifier with approximate CGM mag lats EBR~42, SPT~41, COI~41, CLF~49, HAD~49, NGK~52 (marginal/inside), ESK~53 (marginal), LER~58 (outside), SNK identity-ambiguous, eight of nine are inside-band-or-marginal, one is clearly outside — giving exactly 2 unique predicted values (ensemble-median vs ≤30%), matching the '2–3 unique predicted values' disclosure within the slop introduced by the two marginal stations. Kp for 2017-08-21 17:20 UTC: NOAA/GFZ 3-hourly records show Kp 2+ to 3- in the 15–18 UT interval — 'Kp ≈ 3' phrasing consistent.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: (a) '0.1%/1.8% at seven of nine' FSF precision overstated by ~5× (deferred across four cycles — minor Hole 5 above). (b) ≤30% bound has no specific Kim & Chang figure citation backing it — marked as new moderate hole H1 above. All other numerical claims verified.

tldr_review.notes: Both TLDRs remain accurate simplifications of the expanded content and were not altered by the three self-applied patches. tldr_evidence: 'Eclipses cause small magnetic dips because the moon's shadow reduces ionospheric conductivity — this has been known since Chapman described it in 1933. The dome's observed -10.9 nT at Boulder is exactly what this standard mechanism predicts, to within measurement uncertainty.' Factually correct. tldr_verdict: 'The Chapman ionospheric mechanism, documented since 1933 and quantitatively confirmed by Kim & Chang (2018) across 39 eclipses, predicts this signal without dome parameters. The dome does genuinely derive a station-to-station scaling (FSF = H(r)/r) but it anchors that scaling to the 2017 Boulder observation itself, reusing the same −10.9 nT value as both confirmed WIN and 2026 baseline — a ratio between stations is not a prediction of the signal, and the absolute magnitude is still borrowed from the data.' Factually correct, appropriately simplifies. No TLDR fix required.

deeper_analysis: c9 assesses the three self-applied patches (ISS-1476 Z-component swap, ISS-1484 ≤30% out-of-band quantification, ISS-1485 binary-classifier disclosure) and finds all three close their targeted c8 holes cleanly at the prose-mechanics level. The ISS-1484 patch, however, introduces a fresh moderate hole: the ≤30% upper bound is pre-registered without a specific Kim & Chang figure/table citation, and the paper itself likely does not tabulate a >50°-CGM attenuation factor (per the EXP's own admission that the 39-eclipse binned amplitudes are not currently accessible). This exposes the pre-registration to a 'number pulled from a paper that does not contain it' defender attack and creates a real risk that OUR prediction fails on the 2026 LER residual while the dome's does not. The three minor persisting holes (parallel-drift overclaim, 0.1%/1.8% precision overclaim, 'any smooth monotonic H(r)' assertion) are now deferred across four consecutive cycles (c6, c7, c8, c9) and should be batched into a single cleanup EXP along with the new H1 ≤30%-citation fix. The operative verdict 'Std Model Explains' remains untouched — all of c9's findings tighten or loosen the FUTURE 2026-08-12 pre-registered test rather than re-opening the 2017 non-discrimination argument. Cross-WIN consistency with WIN-025/068 is currently fine because WIN-010 carries more detail (not contradictory detail); the next cleanup EXP should either propagate the methodology consistently (including a decision about whether ≤30% applies to past eclipses) or explicitly scope the ≤30% bound to 2026-08-12.

citations_verified: Chapman (1933, Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr.) — verified in prior cycles; canonical citation for Sq-suppression eclipse mechanism

Kim & Chang (2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2054) — verified in prior cycles; the Y-component increase + X/Z/F decrease finding at 30°–50° mag lat is the paper's headline result. Now cited for the Z-component response envelope (correct after ISS-1476) and for an 'attenuation envelope for latitudes outside the primary response zone' (UNVERIFIED — no specific figure/table named in the parenthetical added by ISS-1484; flagged in H1 above)
citations_unchecked: Kim & Chang (2018) — specific figure/table supporting a ≤30% attenuation factor for >50°-CGM stations. This is the pending verification tied to H1; a follow-up cleanup EXP should name the figure or acknowledge its absence.
IAGA observatory registry — authoritative for codes and geographic coordinates; CGM magnetic latitudes are derived, CGM model year (IGRF epoch) should be named in the eventual 2026 analysis note
